did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning-based U-Net (Convolutional Neural Network) algorithm for skin lesion segmentation and extracted features (color, texture, shape) which were then classified using machine learning models including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Naïve Bayes (NB) to diagnose melanoma from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details a specific methodology involving a U-Net algorithm for segmentation and SVM, RF, KNN, and NB classifiers for diagnosis, followed by 'Experimental Results' reporting new metrics (e.g., 85.19% accuracy for SVM, 77.5% Dice coefficient) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning Based Skin Lesion Segmentation and Classification of Melanoma Using Support Vector Machine (SVM)
journal: Asian Pacific Journal of Cancer Prevention
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve melanoma classification performance by integrating deep learning-based automatic skin lesion segmentation with traditional machine learning classifiers. The researchers utilized a U-Net convolutional neural network to segment dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2016 dataset, followed by the extraction of color, texture, and shape features using methods such as Local Binary Pattern, Edge Histogram, HOG, and Gabor. These extracted features were then fed into Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Naïve Bayes classifiers to distinguish between melanoma and benign lesions. The segmentation process achieved a Dice coefficient of 77.5%, while the SVM classifier demonstrated the highest classification accuracy at 85.19%. The results indicate that the U-Net segmentation algorithm effectively enhances the overall diagnostic performance for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification in the study, distinguished from benign lesions using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2016 dataset.
The negative class in the binary classification task, comprising non-melanoma skin lesions from the ISIC 2016 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion region in dermoscopy images
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a U-Net convolutional neural network to segment the skin lesion from the surrounding healthy skin. The segmentation performance was evaluated using the Dice coefficient (77.5%).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Benign lesions)
all_task_types.details: The primary task is binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and benign skin lesions. Features extracted from segmented images (color, texture, shape) were fed into SVM, Random Forest, KNN, and Naïve Bayes classifiers. The SVM classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 85.19%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 Challenge Dataset (Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection)
datasets_used.description: A public challenge dataset released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the ISBI 2016 conference. It consists of dermoscopic images divided into parts for different tasks (segmentation, feature extraction, classification).

use.training: 900 images from Part 1 were used as training images for segmentation (with ground truth). From the segmented images, 90% (810 images) were used as training data for classification.
use.testing: 900 images from Part 3 were held out as the test dataset for segmentation. The remaining 10% (90 images) of the segmented images were held out as test data for classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1-score, and Dice coefficient.

segmentation.notes: The U-Net algorithm was used for skin lesion segmentation. The Dice coefficient value of 77.5% was achieved. Some text mentions 77.59%, but 77.5% is the primary value cited in the Abstract and Results section.

classification.notes: SVM classifier produced the best result in terms of accuracy and F1-score compared to other classifiers. Naïve Bayes produced the best precision (45.62%). Classification was performed on features extracted from segmented images (color, texture, shape) using LBP, Edge Histogram, HOG, and Gabor methods.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net (Convolutional Neural Network) for segmentation; Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and Naïve Bayes (NB) for classification

architecture_details.segmentation_architecture_description: Combination of deconvolutional network and Fully Connected Network (FCN). Consists of a contracting path and an expansive path. Contracting path follows typical CNN architecture with repeated applications of two 3x3 unpadded convolutions, each followed by a rectified linear unit (ReLU) and a 2x2 maxpooling operation with stride 2 for down sampling. Expansive path consists of upsampling of the feature map followed by 2x2 convolution (up-convolution) that halves the number of feature channels, concatenation with correspondingly cropped feature map from contracting path, and a convolutional and ReLU layer. Final layer uses 1x1 convolution to map each 64 component feature vector to the desired number of classes.
feature_extraction_methods: Color features (Mean, min, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis for R, G, B and L, U, V components)
Local Binary Pattern (LBP) for texture (histogram size 256)
Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) for shape (size 80)

training_details.training_data_split: 900 images from Part 1 used for segmentation training (with ground truth). From segmented images, 90% (810 images) used as training data for classification.
training_details.testing_data_split: 900 images from Part 3 held out as test dataset for segmentation. Remaining 10% (90 images) of segmented images held out as test data for classification.

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Semantic segmentation mask via 1x1 convolution mapping 64-component feature vector to classes

preprocessing_techniques: U-Net based automatic skin lesion segmentation to isolate the lesion region from surrounding healthy skin
Removal of zero values from the RGB matrix components
Normalization of RGB and LUV color components
Color feature extraction (Mean, min, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis) from R, G, B, and L, U, V components
Texture feature extraction using Local Binary Pattern (LBP)
Shape feature extraction using Edge Histogram, Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG), and Gabor filters
L2-norm normalization applied to HOG histogram bins
conclusions: The U-Net segmentation algorithm is identified as the most effective method for skin lesion segmentation in the deep learning environment, achieving a Dice coefficient of 77.5%.

Integrating deep learning-based automatic skin lesion segmentation with traditional machine learning classifiers improves the overall classification performance for melanoma detection compared to using unsegmented images.
Among the tested classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, and Naïve Bayes), the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier demonstrated the highest classification accuracy at 85.19%.

The proposed automated system, which integrates segmentation and classification without manual interaction, effectively assists medical experts in the early diagnosis of melanoma from dermoscopy images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (ISIC 2016) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The classification performance is notably weak, with SVM achieving only 85.19% accuracy, 50% recall, and 46% F1-score, indicating poor sensitivity for melanoma detection.
The segmentation performance is moderate, with a Dice coefficient of 77.5%, which may introduce noise or incomplete lesion boundaries for downstream feature extraction.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals to validate the superiority of the proposed method over classifiers.
There is a discrepancy in reported segmentation metrics between the abstract (77.5%) and the results section (77.59%), suggesting potential reporting inconsistencies.

The feature extraction pipeline uses traditional methods (LBP, HOG, Gabor) on segmented regions rather than end-to-end deep learning, which may limit the capture of complex high-level features compared to modern CNN-based classifiers.

The dataset split uses 900 images for segmentation training and 900 for segmentation testing, but the classification training/test split is derived from the segmented images (90% train, 10% test), raising concerns about data leakage or insufficient independent test sets for robust evaluation.

The study claims the system is 'automated' and 'without manual interaction,' but the reliance on U-Net segmentation means errors in segmentation directly propagate to classification, a critical failure mode not fully addressed.
